zotero.abstractNote: We consider the problem of learning causal representations from high-dimensional data. Specifically, we assume that observed data are generated by a nonlinear mixing of underlying latent variables, which are themselves related via a linear structural causal model (SCM). We show that if we have access to interventional data, we can identify the latent causal variables and the underlying SCM up to a permutation and scaling, even when the mixing function is unknown and highly nonlinear. Our approach relies on the fact that interventions change the distribution of the latent variables in a structured way, which can be exploited to disentangle the latent factors. We provide theoretical guarantees for identifiability and propose a practical algorithm for learning the causal representations. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on synthetic and real-world datasets.
